title: The Geometry Underlying the Quantum Harmonic Oscillator
abstract: We consider two-dimensional harmonic oscillator in the complex Bargmann-Fock-Segal representation with $T^*{\mathbb R}^{2}={\mathbb C}^2$ as classical phase space. We show that the eigenfunctions $ψ_n$ of the quantum Hamiltonian correspond to complex radial coordinates in the reduced phase space ${\mathbb C}^2/{\mathbb Z}_n\subset{\mathbb C}^2$. They describe ${\mathbb Z}_n$-invariant motion of particle along a circle $S^1$ in lens space $S^3/{\mathbb Z}_n\subset{\mathbb C}^2/{\mathbb Z}_n$, where ${\mathbb Z}_n$ is the cyclic group of rotation by an angle $2π/n$ on the circle $S^1$, $n=1,2,...\,$. Thus the general solution of the Schrödinger equation carries information about an infinite number of admissible classical states $ψ_n$ that can be mapped to other states after lifting into the quantum bundle. We show that in the Kepler/hydrogen atom problem there is a similar correspondence between classical and quantum states.
summary: This paper proposes a geometric foundation for quantum mechanics by treating the quantum harmonic oscillator as a gauge theory on an extended phase space. It demonstrates that quantum effects, such as the Heisenberg uncertainty principle and the non-commutativity of operators, are direct consequences of the curvature of a complex line bundle. The author shows that quantum states are holomorphic sections of this bundle, and that the transition from classical to quantum mechanics involves moving from the dynamics of points to the dynamics of sections. The framework is further extended to show a similar geometric correspondence in the hydrogen atom problem.
main_problem: Investigating the underlying geometric and algebraic-geometric structure of the quantum harmonic oscillator to understand the relationship between classical and quantum states.
main_result: The paper establishes that quantum wave functions are holomorphic sections of a complex line bundle, where the non-commutativity of operators (CCR) and the uncertainty principle arise directly from the curvature of the bundle.
method: The author uses geometric quantization, gauge theory, and algebraic geometry (specifically Geometric Invariant Theory and the blow-up technique) to model the oscillator as a particle moving in the total space of a bundle.
model_or_system: A two-dimensional isotropic quantum harmonic oscillator represented in the complex Bargmann-Fock-Segal representation, with a classical phase space of C^2 and a quantum bundle L_v.
key_observables: Creation and annihilation operators, energy levels (E_n), charge density, current, and Majorana stars (zeros of the polynomial part of the wave function).
important_parameters: Mass (m), angular frequency (omega), Planck constant (hbar), and the Chern class (n) of the bundle.
main_assumptions: The vacuum state is assumed to have no external sources; the model assumes the validity of Kustaanheimo-Stiefel and Moser regularizations for the Kepler problem extension.
paper_structure: The paper begins by defining the geometric framework of the oscillator in the Bargmann-Fock-Segal representation, moves to the construction of the quantum bundle and the derivation of CCR from bundle curvature, explores the transition from point-like classical motion to section-based quantum motion using lens spaces, and concludes by extending these geometric principles to the Kepler/hydrogen atom problem.
why_interesting: This paper provides a deep geometric foundation for quantum mechanics, offering a new perspective on the origin of the uncertainty principle and the transition from classical to quantum regimes, which is conceptually relevant to the fundamental understanding of quantum optics and gauge-theoretic approaches to many-body systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
